content.full_text:
91-461

CITY OF SAN PABLO
one alvarado square                                    (415) 215-3009
san pablo, california 94806
(corner of san pablo avenue & church lane)

alvarado adobe

Office of the City Attorney                           FAX (415) 235-7059

October 8, 1991                                       BY FAX


Blanca M. Breeze
Counsel, Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 "J" Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

Re:  Request for Formal Written Advice for Mayor Sharon Brown

Dear Ms. Breeze:

I have been authorized by Mayor Sharon Brown of the City of San
Pablo, to request written advice from the Commission pursuant to
Government Code §83114(b) and Regulation 18329. Mayor Brown's
mailing address is listed above.

The San Pablo City Council will be conducting a public hearing at
the end of this month regarding various land use approvals. After
all testimony is received, the Council will take its actions via
four separate motions and votes on the following items:

1.     Certification of the EIR;

2.     A General Plan Amendment, that would redesignate a 12.8
acre parcel of land from Open Space to Low Density Residential;

3.     A Zoning Ordinance Amendment for the same parcel, from a
Single Family Residential District (R-1) to a Planned Community
District (PC); and

4.     A Conditional Land Use Permit that would allow the
development of 47 single family homes on the affected parcel. The
developer intends to sell these homes at a price range of $200,000,
to $225,000, although it could be higher depending on various costs
and assessments. The value of these homes would generally be
higher than the value of homes surrounding the project site.

The proposed project is located in the Oak Park Redevelopment Area
of the City. Properties to the west of the site are zoned R-2
(Duplex Residential) and are developed by older duplex units and
several new single family homes. Properties to the south are in an

"CITY WITH NEW DIRECTIONS"

[The image appears to be a mostly blank or heavily faded document with very little legible text. Most of the content is too faint or degraded to read accurately. There appear to be some text elements at the top of the page, but they are too illegible to transcribe with certainty.]

Page 2
Request for Formal Written Advice for Mayor Sharon Brown
October 8, 1991


R-1 District and developed by single family dwellings. To the east
is a 53 unit townhouse complex. To the north is a PC District
developed by a relatively new shopping center.

No studies have been done as to whether the proposed subdivision
will result in an increase in the fair market value of the
surrounding properties. The sales prices of the new homes in the
subdivision will generally be higher than sales prices of homes
surrounding the project site. However, some residents feel that
their real estate values will decrease due to the loss of open
space surrounding their homes.

The Council will also be considering the adoption of a
Redevelopment Plan Amendment that would affect the parcel. This
Amendment would simply ensure that the land use designations in the
project area will always be the same as the general plan's
designation of the area. Adoption of the ordinance amending the
redevelopment plan will be scheduled after the City Council
considers the other applications.

Mayor Brown requests advice from the Commission as to whether she
may participate and vote on these items. The Mayor has been a real
estate agent for the past seven years, and a real estate broker for
two years. She is a partner and 50% owner of De Anza Real Estate
and Property Management Company (De Anza). De Anza's offices are
located approximately 9/10 of a mile from the project site, in
unincorporated territory,. De Anza has a long-term lease for the
office.

The Mayor has invested over $10,000 in De Anza, which is not a
retail business. De Anza is not listed on the New York or American
Stock exchanges, or the National Association of Securities Dealers
National Market List. It is not a Fortune 500 corporation. It
has net tangible assets of less than $4,000,000, pre-tax income for
the last fiscal year of less than $750,000, and net income for the
last fiscal year of less than $400,000. Mayor Brown regularly
receives commission income of over $250 per year from De Anza.

Twelve real estate agents work for De Anza, which normally takes a
percentage of their commissions. The company does approximately
15% of its business in the City of San Pablo. Listings from the
sale of single-family homes comprise approximately 70% of De Anza's
business; the property management side of the company brings in
approximately 30%.

Even though this would be a new subdivision, the developer feels
that, because of the anticipated sales prices of the homes, most of
the initial buyers will be represented by realtors. Nevertheless,

Page 3
Request for Formal Written Advice for Mayor Sharon Brown
October 8, 1991


it is highly improbable that, if approved, construction would be
substantially underway and homes offered for sale prior to twelve
months following the City Council's approval.

In light of these facts, we pose the following questions:

     1.     Does Mayor Brown's partnership or business interest in De
Anza Real Estate require her disqualification?

     2.     If Mayor Brown had received commission income of more
than $250.00 in the past year from the sale of real property
located in the area surrounding the project site, would that
require her disqualification?

     3.     Does Mayor Brown's leasehold interest in the De Anza
office require her disqualification?

     4.     Assuming a conflict of interest is deemed to exist, if
the decision on the Use Permit were bifurcated from the other
decisions, would Mayor Brown be allowed to vote on the EIR
Certification, the General Plan Amendment, and/or the zone change,
without a conflict of interest?

     5.     Assuming a conflict of interest is deemed to exist, if
Mayor Brown elected to place the public interest over her personal
interests by announcing her intention to forego all potential
commission revenue resulting from the development, could she then
participate in the decision?  Would the Mayor have to forego such
revenue for one year, two years, or longer?  What process or
procedures would it be necessary to follow to ensure that
participation in the decision is lawful?

If you need any further information, please feel free to call.
Thanks for your consideration.  We look forward to your reply.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Brian M. Libow
                                        City Attorney
BML:sl

cc:  Mayor Sharon Brown
     City Manager

[The image appears to be a blank or nearly blank page with a faint curved line or mark in the upper left portion. The rest of the page shows only aging/discoloration typical of old paper documents. No legible text is visible to transcribe.]
